coredata.dc:title: Effect of the surface chemistry on the photoluminescence properties of boron doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Largitte, Lucie
Travlou, Nikolina A
Florent, Marc
Secor, Jeff
Bandosz, Teresa J.
coredata.dc:description: Two boron-doped carbon dots (BCDs), CABA and TBBA, were prepared from various boron and carbon containing precursors using a bottom-up approach. The BCDs’ chemistry and morphology were evaluated by IR, XPS and TEM analyses. The surface of BCDs was rich in surface functional groups including CB, B–O, CO and CO bonds. Their optical properties were analyzed by UV–vis, excitation and fluorescence spectroscopy. BCDs solutions, when excited at 350 nm, emitted fluorescent light with a maximum at 420−430 nm (in a violet range). The excitation spectra of BCDs were deconvoluted into three components centered respectively at 250, at around 320 and at 350 nm. The excitation peak at 250 nm was linked to the CC bonds, the one around 320 nm - to the CB bonds and the one at 350 nm - to the CO/CO bonds. The calculated quantum yields varied between 1.7 and 9.5 %. The latter number corresponds to one of the best fluorescence quantum yields reported for BCDs. This study highlighted the importance of the surface chemistry and especially of the CB and CO/CO bonds in governing the photoluminescence of BCDs. The highest quantum yield was found for BCDs with a highcontribution of CB and COB bonds and a small contribution of CO bonds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boron doped carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Quantum yield
Surface chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the boron carbon dots, BCDs
Characterization of the chemical and structural features of the carbon dots powders
Characterization of the optical properties of the carbon dots solutions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of the boron doped carbon dots.
Deconvolutions of C 1s, O 1s and B 1s core energy level spectra for TB-BA and CA-BA.

(A) Absorbance spectra of the boron-doped carbon dots solutions. (B) Fluorescence spectra of the boron-doped carbon dots solutions at 350 nm excitation and color of the dots solutions under UV lamp at 365 nm.
Excitation spectra of the boron carbon dots solutions at their maximum wavelength fluorescence (A) and their deconvolution (B).
Fluorescence spectra of CA-BA and TB-BA at various excitation wavelengths.
Procedures used to prepare BCDs and illustration of BCDs’ structures (the differences in the number of specific groups intend to show the differences in the dot's chemistries).

row.entry: 2 mL Triisopropyl borate, 1 g Boric acid, 20 mL Water
1 g Citric acid, 1 g Boric acid, 20 mL Water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The deconvolution of C 1s, O 1s and B 1s core energy level spectra for the BCDs.
Deconvolution results of the excitation peak for CA-BA and TB-BA.

head.ce:title: Effect of the surface chemistry on the photoluminescence properties of boron doped carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two boron-doped carbon dots (BCDs), CABA and TBBA, were prepared from various boron and carbon containing precursors using a bottom-up approach. The BCDs’ chemistry and morphology were evaluated by IR, XPS and TEM analyses. The surface of BCDs was rich in surface functional groups including CB, B–O, CO and CO bonds. Their optical properties were analyzed by UV–vis, excitation and fluorescence spectroscopy. BCDs solutions, when excited at 350 nm, emitted fluorescent light with a maximum at 420−430 nm (in a violet range). The excitation spectra of BCDs were deconvoluted into three components centered respectively at 250, at around 320 and at 350 nm. The excitation peak at 250 nm was linked to the CC bonds, the one around 320 nm - to the CB bonds and the one at 350 nm - to the CO/CO bonds. The calculated quantum yields varied between 1.7 and 9.5 %. The latter number corresponds to one of the best fluorescence quantum yields reported for BCDs. This study highlighted the importance of the surface chemistry and especially of the CB and CO/CO bonds in governing the photoluminescence of BCDs. The highest quantum yield was found for BCDs with a highcontribution of CB and COB bonds and a small contribution of CO bonds.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are spheroid carbon nanoparticles, rich in oxygen-containing surface functional groups (carboxyl, phenol). Their sizes are smaller than 10 nm, they are photoluminescent, non-toxic and are highly stable and biocompatible [1–10]. These features direct their applications in bioimaging, nanomedicine, catalysis and sensing [1–6]. The photoluminescence properties of CDs are usually linked to the quantum confinement effect of their conjugated π domains and consequently to the size of the dot particles and to their surface/edge states [1,6,2–10]. The control of the optical properties of CDs is a main key to their use at an industrial scale.

The chemical properties of CDs can be modified by an addition of heteroatoms which can increase their photoluminescence properties. Since such modifications not always lead to the desired effect, one of the objectives of the paper is to better understand the dots’ optical behavior upon their surface functionalization. In the case of CDs, the doping elements are located close to the carbon in the periodic table. It has been reported that the addition of heteroelements with more electrons than carbon to the CD surface, as nitrogen or sulfur, can markedly affect their electronic properties and thus the optical behavior [6,8,9,11,12]. Concerning the boron addition, a heteroatom with less electrons than carbon, most of the work reported in the literature focuses on a co-doping with boron and nitrogen [13–33], whose effects may overlap or work synergistically. The increase in the number of heteroatoms was reported as leading to an increase in luminescence properties. However, it is still a challenge to understand the effect of each dopant on the luminescence. There is a scarce literature describing only boron doping [34–45]. Therefore, the examination and understanding of the optical properties of CDs doped only with boron, could bring a further insight to the basic science of these materials' properties and this paper focuses on this aspect. The majority of works on BCDs report their characterization and their application in the domains of imaging [35,39], environmental pollution (detection of metals [17,32,34,42,43] and pesticides [29]) and health (detection of biomolecules [13,15,21,26,28,36,37,40,41] and antibiotics [44]). All these applications use the luminescence properties of the dots to detect and quantify chemical compounds, however, the focus on a better understanding of a relation between the dots chemistry and their luminescence properties and on the luminescence mechanism is rather missing. A better control of the luminescence properties of the dots, which is a natural consequence of a better understanding of the luminescence mechanism, could expand the applications of these materials.

The methods used to synthesize CDs are classified in two categories: top-down and bottom-up. In the top-down route, larger carbon structures such as activated carbon or graphene oxide are broken into nanometer size entities using an arc discharge, laser ablation or strong chemical oxidants [6,46]. In the bottom-up route, small carbon containing compounds such as citric acid or citrate are used as precursors of carbon dots in thermal and pressure treatments [6,46]. The latter method offers a better control of the synthesis conditions such as the composition of the precursors. The BCDs are usually prepared from mixtures of a boron agent plus a carbonaceous molecule [34,35,37–39] or, less frequently, directly from a boron-carbon molecule [40]. The routes of synthesis include electrolytic synthesis [34], arc discharge [38], microwave [31], hydrothermal or solvothermal process [22,37,39,40,44]. Among them, the bottom-up route by hydrothermal process is most often used.

While some research results focusing on the preparation and application of BCDs have already been published, the role of the specific surface chemistry of the dots (specific surface functional groups) and its effects on the luminescence properties have not been analyzed in details. In this study, citric acid with boric acid or triisopropyl borate are used to prepare BCDs following the hydrothermal process. The objective of this paper is to investigate, compare and understand the optical properties of these two boron doped carbon dots (BCDs) obtained from various precursors following a bottom-up synthesis method. Since water is the only solvent used, this method is a more ecofriendly synthesis route than the top-down chemical oxidation one. The preparation conditions were expected to lead to BCDs of diverse chemical and photoluminescence features, interrelated to each other. An attempt is made to link the photoluminescence properties of two BCDs characterized by UV–vis, excitation and fluorescence spectroscopy to their surface chemical features analyzed by IR, XPS and TEM.

All chemicals (citric acid- from Spectrum, boric acid, triisopropyl borate and sodium hydroxide-from Alfa Aesar, hydrochloric acid 36.5 % -from VWR and dichloromethane- from BDH) were an AR grade and used as-received. Water was purified in an Ipure® purification column, and then, an additional purification on an ion-exchange resin (Vision 125 deionizer) was carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the boron carbon dots, BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 presents the summary of synthesis procedures used to obtain the boron-carbon dots (BCDs) addressed in this paper. Two precursors were used to prepare BCDs: citric acid (CA) and triisopropyl borate (TB). Citric acid is a carbon precursor while triisopropyl borate contains both boron and carbon arranged in COB bonds. A mixture of each precursor with boric acid (BA) as a boron source was prepared as described in Table 1. As anticipated interactions leading to the incorporation of boron is the addition reaction of boric acid with citric acid and condensation reaction of triisopropyl borate with boric acid. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and kept in a Thermolyne 6000 furnace at 200 °C at a heating-rate 3 °C/min and held for 5 h. The BCDs obtained from those precursors were referred to as: CA-BA and TB-BA. In the names used, the first group of letters corresponds to the name of the precursor of BCDs and the second group of letters indicates the boron source which was added during the preparation process.

After cooling to a room temperature, the BCDs solutions were first filtrated using a syringe equipped with a 0.2 μm cylindrical membrane nylon filter, and then neutralized by adding NaOH pellets or a concentrated HCl solution, under stirring. The neutralization step was used since the pH of the dot solution can affect the fluorescence results [40] and therefore the properties of BCDs could be compared at the same pH ∼ 7. The obtained carbon dot solutions were washed 3 times with dichloromethane, filtrated again and centrifuged. After the centrifugation, the BCDs solutions were filtrated one last time using the 0.2 μm cylindrical membrane nylon filter, aiming to remove the remaining solid particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the chemical and structural features of the carbon dots powders
ce:section.ce:para: Each carbon quantum dot solution was heated in an oven at 120 °C to evaporate water and to obtain a BCDs powder. The surface chemical and structural features of the powdered dots were analyzed using Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometry (XPS), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM).

FTIR spectra were collected on a Nicolet 380 FT-IR spectrometer using an attenuated total reflectance method (ATR). The spectra were collected 64 times and corrected for a background noise. The experiments were conducted on powdered samples without the addition of KBr.

The XPS analysis was carried out on a Physical Electronics PHI 5000 VersaProbe II spectrometer. Al Kα X-ray radiation (50 W, 15 kV, 1486.6 eV) was used as an excitation source. High resolution spectra were recorded at a take-off angle of 45° by using a concentric hemispherical analyzer operating in a constant-pass-energy mode, at 29.35 eV, with a 200 μm diameter analysis area.
TEM/HRTEM images were collected on a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The analyses were conducted on dry powdered dots, previously dispersed in ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the optical properties of the carbon dots solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of the carbon dots solutions were recorded on an Evolution 300 UV–vis spectrophotometer, Thermoscientific. A quartz Suprasil cuvette was used and a baseline was corrected using a cuvette with water. The fluorescence and the excitation spectra of the carbon dots solutions were collected using a 90 degree scattering geometry in a FluoroMax-3 fluorescence spectrometer from Horiba Jobin Yvon. Excitation and emission slit widths were of 2 nm resolution. All measured spectra were corrected to the lamp output power. The fluorescence spectra after excitation at different wavelengths between 320 and 400 nm were recorded. The excitation spectra were recorded for an emission wavelength of 420 nm for TB-BA, 430 nm for CA-BA. This wavelength corresponds to the maximum intensity of fluorescence obtained in the fluorescence spectrum for the 350 nm excitation.

The quantum yield (QY) of each BCD was calculated from the following equation:
(1)
Q Y
B C D
=
Q Y
s u l f a t e
q u i n i n e
×
A
s u l f a t e
q u i n i n e
A
B C D
×
F
B C D
F
s u l f a t e
q u i n i n e
×
η
B C D
s o l v e n t
η
s u l f a t e
q u i n i n e
s o l v e n t

In this equation, QY is the quantum yield, η - the refractive index, F - the area of the fluorescence peak and A – the absorbance. Quinine sulfate in 0.05 M sulfuric acid (QY = 0.546) was used as a standard. The quantum yield measurements were conducted using a 350 nm excitation. Since either for BCDs or quinine sulfate, water was used as a solvent, the refractive index was equal to 1.33.

The FTIR spectra were collected to evaluate the surface chemistry of BCDs and to verify whether or not the synthesis led to two BCDs of diverse chemical features. As seen in Fig. 1 , the spectra of the dots revealed various bands (up to fourteen bands are revealed in the spectra). Based on the objective of this work, the bands related to boron, carbon and oxygen moieties were targeted. Therefore, the well-resolved band at about 1190 cm−1 was linked to the CB bonds [16,17,47] and/or CO bonds [48]. The intensity of this band on the spectrum of TB-BA was higher than in that of CA-BA. The broad band between 1300 and 1600 cm−1 is likely associated to the overlapping of IR bands corresponding to BO bonds (∼ 1450 cm−1) [16,17,48], BOH (∼ 1350 cm−1) [40,13] and carboxyl-carbonates (∼ 1500 cm−1) [48]. The presence of OH is clearly seen as an intensive band at 3220 cm-1 [48]. The CO bonds of alcohols, phenols and esters are expected to contribute to the bands between 1100- 1200 cm-1 [13,47,48]. Generally, all bands of TB-BA are more intense than those of CA-BA. It may be due to a lower absorption coefficient for TB-BA than CA-BA and/or to the differences in the penetration depth of the samples in TB-BA compared to that in CA-BA. The differences between the samples are the most visible in the bands at ∼ 1300 cm−1, which are revealed only in the spectrum of TB-BA. This is the range of an ether bond absorption, COC [48], and/or of that of a COB bond [49] and also the range where BC bonds in sp2 configurations vibrate [47]. The main vibration of BC in sp2 was expected to be detected at about 1200 cm−1. The bands intensity in this region in the spectrum of TB-BA suggest that this sample had a higher contribution of BC bonds in the sp2 configuration than had CA-BA. Indeed, the intensity ratio between the band at 1200 cm−1 and the one at 1151 cm−1 was 1.6 for TB-BA and 1.4 for CA-BA. Moreover, various bands showing CH vinyl bands (bands at 814 and 943 cm−1 in Fig. 1) and CH aromatic bands are also seen in the FTIR spectra of CA-BA and TB-BA (bands at 639, 700 and 883 cm−1 in Fig. 1).

XPS analysis was carried out on TB-BA and CA-BA to deepen the study of their chemical and structural features. The results are presented in Fig. 2 and Table 2 . Both samples contain boron, oxygen and carbon in similar quantities. The very high content of oxygen suggests a marked contribution of borates /boric acid. This agrees with the deconvolution results of O 1s and B 1s core energy level spectra. The former shows a very high contribution of oxygen in BO bonds in the TB-BA sample (45.9 %). The latter indicates that ∼ 75 % of boron is in boron-oxygen configurations. The higher contribution of BO bonds in TB-BA than that in CA-BA suggests that in the former sample most of the CO bonds are COB bonds. The deconvolution results of C 1s core energy level spectra indicate that TB-BA has a higher contribution of carbon in CB bonds compared to that of CA-BA and this is in agreement with the FTIR analysis. Even though in TB-BA, the CO bonds are detected, their contributions are much smaller than those in CA-BA. It implies that the carbon phase of CA-BA is more oxidized than that of TB-BA. This might be also indirectly related to less CB bonds in the sp2 configuration in this sample. More carbon in CA-BA than that in TB-BA can lead to more delocalized π electrons in the former sample. This is supported by a contribution of a π- π∗ satellite peak in the C 1s core energy level spectrum of CA-BA. The presence of boron oxides suggests a hybrid nature of synthetized BCDs where, besides the pure BCD phase, a boron-based phase might exist and it might be linked with BCDs via dispersive interactions or via COB bonds. This has already been observed for other BCDs described in the literature [35,36].

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the boron carbon dots solutions are presented in Fig. 3 A. CA-BA absorbs in a wider wavelengths range than does TB-BA and the absorbance intensity for CA-BA until 400 nm is always higher than that for TB-BA. It could be due to a higher concentration of dots in CA-BA sample and/or due to the specific chemical features of the CA-BA dots. The absorbance at 270 nm is 0.2 and 5 for TB-BA and CA-BA, respectively. The spectrum of CA-BA reveals two peaks between 270-280 nm. At wavelengths longer than 280 nm the absorbance intensity decreases. For TB-BA, only a gradual decrease in the absorbance is seen with a shoulder between 230−280 nm. This behavior is not unusual and has been reported for other dots [36,38]. The peaks around 230−280 nm are usually attributed to the transition π-π* in the CC bond [34,35,39]. Indeed, the general range of the absorbance of the double carbon bond is from 170 nm to 250 nm [50]. Moreover, various bands showing not only CH vinyl (CCH) bands but also CH aromatic (CCCCH) bands are also seen on the FTIR spectra of CA-BA and TB-BA (bands at 943 and 883 cm−1 in Fig. 1), supporting the existence of the CC bonds in these dots.

Fig. 3B presents the fluorescence spectra of the carbon dots' solutions at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. All spectra reveal only one emission peak with the maximum localized in the violet range. For CA-BA, the maximum is centered at 430 nm while for TB-BA the emission peak is centered at 420 nm. Under sunlight, the carbon dots solutions are colorless, and under a UV lamp at 365 nm, the BCDs solution of TB-BA appears as dark blue while that of CA-BA has a bright blue color. It might be linked to the intensity of the fluorescence emission of each dot at this specific wavelength of excitation. The shape of the fluorescence peak is similar for both BCDs. However, the FWHM of the fluorescence peak of CA-BA is larger (∼105 nm) than the one of TB-BA (∼91 nm), following the same order as the absorbance intensity.

There are reports in the literature suggesting that the broadness of the fluorescence peaks increases with an increase in the size of the carbon dots [50–52], however, it is not the trend found in this study. The emission spectrum of CA-BA exhibits the broadest peak, while that of TB-BA, the narrowest one. This would suggest that CA-BA dots are larger in size than are TB-BA dots. The TEM analysis of these two samples indicates the opposite trend (Fig. 4 ). The size of the dots increases in the following order: CA-BA < TB-BA. While CA-BA dots are spherical, having sizes around 4.5 nm, those of TB-BA are spheroid with sizes of around 6 nm. Thus, in this study, the larger is the dot size, the smaller is the fluorescence peak area. Since the differences are rather small, the effect of the shape, which in fact can be related to dots agglomeration or to the dots composition, cannot be excluded.

Some studies also reported that the wavelength at the maximum fluorescence intensity increases with the size of the dot [50,51] and such an effect was also not found in this case. Other studies on boron carbon dots reported that excitation at 340 nm led to one fluorescence peak at wavelengths of 420 nm [40], 430 nm [38], 450 nm [36], 530 nm [34] or 600−700 nm [39]. Shan and coworkers [36] reported an emission peak at 370 nm for boron-doped CDs prepared in an organic solution, however the precise excitation wavelength was not mentioned. To our knowledge, Fan and coworkers [35] are the only researchers who reported two emission peaks for boron-doped CDs, at 440 and 550 nm, at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The wavelength range of the fluorescence peak, in the violet range, for both synthetized boron doped carbon dots of this study is in agreement with many of the previously reported results [35,36,38,40]. However, the reasons of the variety of fluorescent peaks positions (in UV, in violet, in green and even in red) found in the literature on BCDs is still under discussion. As reported, such differences might be related to the hybridization state of –B (sp2 or sp3) [10], the quantity of –B [8,10], the presence of other heteroelements in the carbon dot structure [6–8,10], the acidity [1,6,7,10] and the size of the dots [6–8,10]. All of these can affect the fluorescence features.

Since the photoluminescence characteristics of the dots of this study show little enhancement with a decrease in the dots' size, surface chemistry might play a predominant role and the effect of this feature needs to be extensively discussed. For this, a detailed look at the TB-BA and CA-BA features is needed since their fluorescence pattern differ. FTIR analysis suggests the presence of the COB and BC in the sp2 configurations for both samples. Moreover, TB-BA has a higher contribution of carbon in the CB bonds and COB bonds and the surface of its carbon phase has a lower oxidation level than has that of CA-BA (less the CO bonds). It also has the marked contributions of boron in the BO and BOH bonds. Based on these, the existence of C-BO or CB(OH)2 bonds cannot be crossed out. This emphasizes the important role of the surface chemistry to interpret the photoluminescence of the dots and especially that of the contribution of the CB and CO/CO bonds.

Fig. 5 A presents the photoluminescence excitation spectrum of TB-BA (giving an emission at 420 nm) and the one of CA-BA (giving an emission at 430 nm). 420 or 430 nm corresponds to the maximum intensity of the fluorescence peaks for each sample at the 350 nm excitation. The study of the excitation spectra permits the determination of the energetic levels leading to fluorescence. The TB-BA sample once again shows a different behavior than does CA-BA. While on the spectra of CA-BA, one excitation peak centered in the range of 320−360 nm is visible, TB-BA shows two excitation peaks centered at around 250 and 320 nm. The peak at ∼ 250 nm has a higher intensity and it might be related to the transition π-π* in the C = C bond. Since the excitation peaks are localized below 320 nm for TB-BA and below 360 nm for CA-BA, the maximum intensity of luminescence should be observed for a wavelength of excitation inferior to 320 nm for TB-BA and inferior to 360 nm for CA-BA. The positions of the excitation peaks are in agreement with those of Chen and coauthors [34] who reported two excitation peaks at 270 and 360 nm and with those of Dey and coworkers [38] who also found two excitation peaks at 270 and 320 nm for their boron doped carbon dots. The contributions at around 320 and 360 nm are usually attributed to the transitions n-π*, n-σ* in the carbon heteroatom bonds [34,38]. Since CA-BA and TB-BA are prepared from the precursors containing only carbon, oxygen and boron, the bonds must correspond to CO (carbonyl, carboxyl, ester), CB (CBC, CBO) and CO (COC, COB, OCO and COH carboxylic/phenol/alcohol). Based on the XPS and FTIR analyses and thus on the differences in samples' chemistry, the contributions centered at 250 nm might be related to CC bonds, at around 320 nm -to CB bonds, and at around 360 nm - to CO/CO ones.

The excitation spectra for each sample were deconvoluted into two components, however the ratios of the component areas and the positions of the maxima differ. The single excitation peak centered in the range 320−360 nm for CA-BA has two major contributions with maxima at 320 and 350 nm (Fig. 5B and Table 3 ). The square of a regression coefficient is 0.999 and the area of the latter contribution at 350 nm is larger than that of the former one. In the case of TB-BA, the deconvolution led to the major contributions centered at 250 and 310 nm. It means that the energetic levels which are excited at the maximum wavelength fluorescence correspond to CC and CB bonds for TB-BA and CB and CO/C = O bonds for CA-BA. Note that for TB-BA the CB bond is assigned to 310 nm and for CA-BA - at 320 nm. The maximum of the fluorescence peak is 420 nm for TB-BA and 430 nm for CA-BA. It seems that the position of the maximum fluorescence wavelength changes with the position of the energetic level of CB and a shift between the both is around 110 nm. The peak area in the deconvolution results may be linked to the content of each bond. It is the first time at our knowledge that deconvolutions are used to clearly identify the excitation levels in the excitation spectra.

Fig. 6 presents the fluorescence spectra of BCDs under different excitation wavelengths from 320 to 400 nm. The same behavior is observed for all BCDs tested. They all reveal an excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peak is red-shifted and the intensity of the peak decreases rapidly. For TB-BA, the maximum intensity of fluorescence is obtained for a 320 nm excitation while for CA-BA for 320–340 nm excitations.

The excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior is usually observed for all dots [1–10]. It is related either to the distribution of different sized dots in the sample and/or to the surface chemistry of the dots [34,35,40]. The latter explanation seems more convincing in the case of the samples addressed in this study since several energetic levels from 300 to 350 nm, relative to the CO/CO and CB bonds, have been found for these dots. The decreasing intensity and shift of the emission peaks may correspond to the various CB, CO, CO bonds which are successively excited and then relaxed, when the excitation wavelength increases. The luminescence takes a different pathway following the excited functionalities. When the CO levels are excited, the BCDs may emit a fluorescence at a different energy and intensity than when their CB or CO levels are excited.

The fluorescence quantum yields were calculated for the BCDs solutions. The quantum yield quantifies the number of emitted photons relative to the number of absorbed photons (Eq. 1). The quantum yield of TB-BA is 9.5 % and 2.2 % for CA-BA. The quantum yields measured for the dots of this study are in general agreement with those reported in the literature [34,35,38–40]. The quantum yields found for boron doped carbon dots prepared using electrolysis of graphite rod and borax were between 3.6 and 9.3 %, respectively [34,35]. Dey and collaborators [38] prepared BCDs from graphite and diborane or boron powder stuffed electrode by an electric arc and the quantum yields were 2.3 and 2.5 %. Sadhanala and Nanda used a hydrothermal treatment of sucrose and boric acid and obtained CDs with yields of 0.5 and 2.7 % [39]. Shen and Xia prepared BCDs from boronic acid in a hydrothermal process and the quantum yield was 8.4 % [40]. Shan and coworkers [37] prepared bromide boron carbon dots from hydroquinone and boron bromide by a solvothermal process with a quantum yield of 14.8 %. However, the authors did not address the contribution of bromine in the luminescence properties of their dots. Moreover, some of their dots were prepared in an ethanol solution (C2H5OH is a carbon containing compound) whereas the dots of this study were prepared in an aqueous solution. Recent studies have shown that an increase in time in the hydrothermal process between 8–24 h along with the lower ratios of a carbonaceous compound to a boron agent (between 5 and 14) led to luminescent BCDs obtained from citric acid and boric acid and from citric acid and tetraphenyl borate with quantum yields 30.9 and 42 %, respectively[44,45]. No investigation to link the obtained results to the specific chemistry of the dots was done.

The fluorescence quantum yield of TB-BA is higher than that of CA-BA and it does not follow the trend of the absorbance intensity or the one of the fluorescence peak areas. It rather follows the trends in surface chemistry and highlights the importance of the CC bonds, the CO/CO bonds and the CB bonds in sp2 configurations to explain the photoluminescence mechanism of the carbon dots of this study. Indeed, TB-BA are the dots with the highest quantum yield. They are also the only dots with two excitation peaks at around 250 and 320 nm and the ones which have a higher contribution of COB and CB bonds and the smaller contribution of CO bonds.

The structure of the carbon dots can be described as small clusters of sp2 carbon layer(s) with chemical groups localized on the basal plane or at the edge of the carbon matrix. In addition to the CC bonds from the carbon core or the CC bonds of the carbenes which can be present at the edge, the chemical groups on CA-BA and TB-BA are CO, CO and CB. The fluorescence can be caused either by these chemical groups that are electronically coupled to the graphene core sheets or by a quantum confinement effect of the conjugated π domains of the graphene core [1,6–10]. The photoluminescence properties are usually linked to the relaxation of electrons from excited states to a ground state [10,51]. Then, in the case of the carbon dots, the photoexcited electrons can relax through either the sp2 levels of the graphene core or through the surface states or a combination of the both [51]. The photoluminescence mechanism of the dots of this study can be explained only with the relaxation via the surface states. Indeed, CA-BA possesses a C-B level localized at 320 nm and CO/CO levels at around 350 nm. Following the excitation wavelength, for CA-BA, the electron relaxes going from the C-B energetic level to the CO/ CO energetic levels and finally fluoresces at 430 nm. The photoluminescence mechanism is different for TB-BA which possesses an additional level localized at 250 nm and identified as a CC level. Following the excitation wavelength, the electron relaxes going from the CC level to the CB energetic level located at 310 nm then to the CO/ CO energetic levels and finally fluoresces at 420 nm. The lower wavelength of fluorescence for TB-BA consecutive to the shift of the CB level might be explained by a stronger resonance between the CC, the CO and the CB bands because of a higher amount of COB bonds in the sample and because, for TB-BA, the CO amount is smaller than in CABA. Indeed, the CB and the CO bonds can be considered as radiative centers of fluorescence while the CO bonds are non-radiative or even inhibitive fluorescence centers. This depreciative effect of CO bonds has been also seen for dots of other studies [2,7–10]. The photoluminescence mechanism suggested for CABA and TBBA can explain also the excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior of the dots. The response of the dots to the excitation, in terms of luminescence, differs depending on the functional groups and on the amount of the functional groups which are excited.

Two hybrid boron doped carbon dots (BCDs), CABA and TBBA were prepared from citric acid, triisopropyl borate and boric acid, using the bottom-up route. While both BCDs contain the same elements, their surface chemistry differ, which affects their optical properties. Under the same excitation, CABA has its fluorescence emission shifted to a higher wavelength than has TB-BA. And the latter has a much higher quantum yield (9.5 %, one of the best reported in the literature) compared to 1.7 % for CABA. This was linked to the higher contribution of CB bonds in sp2 configurations and COB bonds in TBBA. That sample had also the smaller contribution of CO bonds, CO being considered as a non-radiative or even an inhibitive fluorescence center.

The results collected highlighted the importance of surface chemistry on the photoluminescence properties of the dots. They indicated that the location, the broadness of the fluorescence peak and the excitation dependent photoluminescence behavior of these dots did not depend on their size but rather on their surface chemistry. Three excitation levels at 250 nm, at around 320 nm and 350 nm were clearly identified. They were attributed to the dots CC states, CB and C-O/CO states. When the electrons were excited to these different excitation states, they relaxed and one fluorescence peak at various wavelengths and intensities was observed. Although this study presents some contribution to a better understanding of the luminescence properties of the dots, some questions still remain. The next step could be to quantify the fluorescence yield of each functional group in order to optimize the synthesis of the carbon dots with regards to their targeted applications in bioimaging and sensing.

Lucie Largitte: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Nikolina A Travlou: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Marc Florent: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Jeff Secor: Data curation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Teresa J. Bandosz: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.
